description:
You are working in the account “user” (home directory = /home/user).

    Background  
    -----------
    A developer has a small web-application project under  
    /home/user/projects/webapp.  
    They now want to catalogue every Python source file that lives inside  
    /home/user/projects/webapp/src (and all of its sub-directories) into a
    lightweight SQLite database so that future build-tools can query it quickly.
    After the catalogue is built, the developer also wants a human-readable JSON
    export and a one-line operation log.

    Your objectives  
    ---------------
    1. Create the SQLite data-base /home/user/projects/project_files.db with a
       single table named files that has the exact schema shown here:

          CREATE TABLE files (
              id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
              filename    TEXT    NOT NULL,   -- e.g. “app.py”
              filepath    TEXT    NOT NULL,   -- absolute path
              sha256      TEXT    NOT NULL,   -- full 64-char hex digest, lowercase
              size_bytes  INTEGER NOT NULL    -- size of the file in bytes
          );

    2. Recursively scan ONLY the directory /home/user/projects/webapp/src for
       files whose names end in “.py” (case-sensitive).  
       • Insert one row per file into the files table.  
       • filename must contain just the base-name (no directory part).  
       • filepath must be the absolute POSIX path (starting with “/”).  
       • sha256 is the SHA-256 digest of the complete file contents, written as
         64 lowercase hexadecimal characters.  
       • size_bytes is the exact byte-length of the file.

    3. After every row has been inserted, export the whole table – sorted
       alphabetically by the filename column – to the file
       /home/user/projects/file_index.json using the following exact format:

          [
            {
              "filename": "app.py",
              "filepath": "/abs/path/to/app.py",
              "sha256": "xxxxxxxx…",
              "size_bytes": 123
            },
            {
              "filename": "config.py",
              "filepath": "/abs/path/to/config.py",
              "sha256": "yyyyyyyy…",
              "size_bytes": 456
            }
          ]

       Formatting rules that WILL be automatically tested:  
       • JSON must be UTF-8, human-readable, and “pretty-printed”
         using exactly 2 spaces for indentation.  
       • Array opening bracket “[” on its own line, closing bracket “]” on its
         own line.  
       • Objects separated by a single comma and a newline.  
       • Keys ordered exactly as: filename, filepath, sha256, size_bytes.  
       • No trailing comma after the last object.  
       • A final newline at the very end of the file is optional.

    4. Create the log file /home/user/projects/operation.log that contains ONE
       single line in the exact form (the timestamp is the current local time,
       24-hour clock; test-code will allow any valid timestamp):

          YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS Processed N files into /home/user/projects/project_files.db

       • Replace YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS with the current time.  
       • Replace N with the total number of .py files you inserted (e.g. “3”).  
       • The path must appear exactly as shown.  
       • No extra spaces before the newline; the file ends with a single “\n”.

    What you can assume is already present  
    --------------------------------------
    • The sqlite3 command-line client is installed.  
    • /home/user/projects/webapp/src already exists and contains three Python
      files that need cataloguing.  No other “.py” files are present anywhere
      else under /home/user/projects/webapp/src.  
    • You have normal user permissions; do NOT use sudo.

    Verification hints  
    ------------------
    • The automated tests will open project_files.db, run
      “SELECT filename, filepath, sha256, size_bytes FROM files ORDER BY
      filename;” and compare the records against the real files on disk.  
    • They will also validate the JSON file character-for-character, so follow
      the required ordering and indentation strictly.  
    • The log line will be evaluated with a regular expression – only the
      timestamp may vary.

    You may use any standard Linux command-line tools (find, xargs, sqlite3,
    openssl, sha256sum, jq, python3, etc.).  
    Display reasonable console output so a human can follow what is happening,
    but keep it concise.  
    Remember: create the database, populate it, generate JSON, and write the
    single-line log.
truth:
<!--
  The verification harness will rely on ALL of the following details.

  1. Pre-existing directory tree and file contents
     ---------------------------------------------
     /home/user/projects/webapp/src/app.py
        Content (exact, incl. newline):   print('Hello World')\n
     /home/user/projects/webapp/src/utils/helpers.py
        Content:                          def add(a, b):\n    return a + b\n
     /home/user/projects/webapp/src/config.py
        Content:                          DEBUG = True\n

     No other *.py files are present under /home/user/projects/webapp/src.

  2. Derived data that MUST be inside the data-base & JSON
     -----------------------------------------------------
     The test-suite will re-calculate the following values at runtime:

       | filename            | sha256 (lowercase)                    | size |
       |---------------------|---------------------------------------|------|
       | app.py              | SHA256 of "print('Hello World')\n"    | 20   |
       | config.py           | SHA256 of "DEBUG = True\n"            | 14   |
       | helpers.py          | SHA256 of "def add(a, b):\n    return a + b\n"| 34 |

     Absolute file paths will be:
       /home/user/projects/webapp/src/app.py
       /home/user/projects/webapp/src/config.py
       /home/user/projects/webapp/src/utils/helpers.py

     IMPORTANT: helpers.py appears in the JSON as "helpers.py", not "utils/helpers.py",
     because the filename column contains only the base-name.

  3. Expected final artefacts
     ------------------------
     a) /home/user/projects/project_files.db
        • Contains exactly one table “files”.
        • Table row-count = 3.
        • Column order and types exactly match the schema given in the public task.

     b) /home/user/projects/file_index.json
        • Pretty-printed with 2-space indentation,
          newlines between array items, closing bracket on its own line.
        • Sorted alphabetically by filename (app.py, config.py, helpers.py).

     c) /home/user/projects/operation.log
        • Single line matching the regex:
          ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} Processed 3 files into /home/user/projects/project_files.db\n$
  -->
name: task_000000_a08fd4cd
